content.text: Provide a succinct summary of this paper. What conclusions are drawn for the training and behavior of LLMs. https://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.03172

**"Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts"** (Liu et al., Stanford/UC Berkeley/Samaya AI, 2023)

**Core Finding**

Performance degrades significantly when relevant information is placed in the middle of a long input context. Models perform best when relevant information appears at the very beginning or end — a distinctive U-shaped curve driven by primacy and recency biases.

**Key Results**

- GPT-3.5-Turbo's multi-document QA performance can drop by more than 20% — in the worst case, performance with 20–30 documents is actually *lower* than its closed-book (no documents) baseline of 56.1%.
- Extended-context models (e.g., GPT-3.5-Turbo 16K vs. 4K, Claude-1.3 100K vs. 8K) show nearly identical performance curves — bigger context windows don't mean better use of that context.
- Model performance saturates long before retrieval recall saturates — using 50 retrieved documents instead of 20 only marginally improves performance (~1.5% for GPT-3.5-Turbo, ~1% for Claude-1.3).

**Architectural Findings**

- Encoder-decoder models are relatively robust to position changes within their training-time sequence length, but show the same U-shaped curve on sequences longer than seen during training.
- Query-aware contextualization (placing the query both before and after documents) enables near-perfect performance on synthetic retrieval tasks, but minimally changes trends on real QA.
- Even base models without instruction fine-tuning exhibit the U-shaped curve, suggesting this is a fundamental training/architecture issue rather than an artifact of RLHF.

**Conclusions for LLM Training & Behavior**

The paper implies several things for practitioners:

1. **Don't assume long context = full context use.** Models are not robustly attending to their entire context window during inference.
2. **Document ordering matters enormously** in RAG pipelines — put the most relevant content first or last, never bury it in the middle.
3. **Throwing more retrieved documents at a model has diminishing returns** and can actively hurt performance.
4. **Evaluating long-context models requires testing performance across all positions**, not just average accuracy — best- vs. worst-case position gap is a meaningful benchmark metric the field had been ignoring.
How does the u shaped attention vary with context size. Is there any speculacy on the size/maximum front and end are?
How does the u shaped attention vary with context size. Is there any speculacy on the size/maximum front and end are?
The paper's fetched content covers this partially but let me grab the rest for the specifics.

The PDF is truncating at the same point. Based on what's available plus the figures described, here's what the paper does and doesn't say:

**How the U-shape changes with context size**

The paper tests three context sizes — 10, 20, and 30 documents (~2K, ~4K, ~6K tokens) — and the U-shape is present at all three sizes. The key observation from Figure 5 is that the *trough gets worse* as context grows: the performance gap between best- and worst-case positions widens with more documents. At 10 docs, middle performance is bad; at 30 docs, it can fall below closed-book baseline entirely.

**On the "safe zone" size at the front and end**

The paper is notably silent on this. It doesn't quantify how many tokens constitute the privileged front/end zones — it only establishes that the effect is positional and relative, not absolute. The data points are coarse (document-level granularity, not token-level), so the exact boundary of where "beginning" ends and "middle" begins isn't characterized.

What can be inferred from the figures: the drop-off appears sharp and begins almost immediately after the first ~1-2 documents, and the recovery at the end is similarly steep — suggesting the safe zones are quite narrow, likely just the first and last document or two regardless of total context size.

**No speculation on mechanism either**

The paper explicitly frames its investigation into *why* this happens as "preliminary" — it identifies that the phenomenon predates instruction fine-tuning (base models show it too) and that decoder-only architecture is implicated, but doesn't go further into what attention patterns are responsible or whether the zone sizes are fixed in tokens vs. relative proportions of total context.

So to directly answer: the U-shape sharpens with longer contexts, but the paper doesn't bound or quantify the safe zones, and offers no speculation on whether they're fixed-size or scale with context length. That's an open question the paper itself leaves on the table as future work.
Is there any further research? It's been a year

content.text:
Yes, quite a lot has happened. Here's the landscape organized by thread:

---

**The "why" was finally explained (2025)**

The biggest development is that the architectural root cause is now understood. A paper titled "Lost in the Middle at Birth" provides the first exact, closed-form mathematical proof that the U-shape is a fundamental topological constraint of the decoder-only architecture itself — present even at random initialization, before any training. Causal masking forces a logarithmic divergence of gradient influence at the start of the prompt (primacy), while residual connections create an isolated O(1) anchor at the final token (recency). Between these lies a "factorial dead zone" where middle-context retrieval is structurally hostile.

The core insight: tokens at the beginning of the context get attended to by every subsequent token. Token #1 is visible to tokens #2, #3, #4... all the way to the end. Token #500, in the middle, is only visible to tokens #501 onward — so earlier tokens accumulate more attention weight simply because they have more opportunities to be attended to.

Critically, empirical validation showed that untrained architectures like Qwen2 already exhibit this U-shape at Step 0, and that the topology is mathematically identical regardless of whether RoPE positional encoding is present or absent.

---

**Proposed fixes**

Several mitigation approaches have emerged:

- Anthropic found that Claude 2.1's long-context performance could be dramatically improved with a simple prompt nudge — adding "Here is the most relevant sentence in the context" boosted accuracy from 27% to 98%.
- MIT/Google's "Found in the Middle" paper (2024) proposed a calibration mechanism to disentangle relevance from position, allowing models to attend to relevant context regardless of where it appears.
- A NeurIPS 2024 paper introduced Multi-scale Positional Encoding (Ms-PoE), a plug-and-play approach that modifies RoPE scaling ratios per attention head to relieve long-term decay effects — no fine-tuning required.

---

**Does the problem still exist in frontier models?**

Mixed picture. A Google paper found that Gemini 2.5 Flash can answer needle-in-a-haystack questions with high accuracy regardless of document position, even near the input context limit — suggesting the effect may be substantially reduced in the most recent models for simple factoid retrieval.

However, a 2024 benchmark (LONGPIBENCH) found that while most current models are more robust against the basic lost-in-the-middle issue, noticeable biases still emerge related to the *spacing* between multiple relevant information pieces — a more complex real-world scenario the original paper didn't test.

---

**Alternative theoretical framing**

One 2025 paper offers an interesting alternative perspective: rather than treating the U-shape purely as a model flaw, it argues it may be an *emergent rational adaptation* to training demands — analogous to the primacy/recency effects in human memory. They show the effect emerges in autoregressive RNNs but not bidirectional encoder-decoders, suggesting both the task demand and causal-style processing are necessary conditions.

---

**Bottom line:** The phenomenon is now theoretically understood (it's baked into the decoder-only architecture from birth), newer frontier models have improved substantially on simple retrieval, but it hasn't been fully solved — especially for complex multi-document scenarios. The practical fix of putting important content first/last still holds.

content.text: This is equivalent to recency bias, right?

Partially, but they're distinct in an important way.

**Recency bias** (in the human cognition sense) is specifically the tendency to over-weight recent/last information. The lost-in-the-middle phenomenon has *two* privileged zones — beginning and end — which is why the paper frames it as primacy *and* recency bias together, producing the U-shape.

The recency component maps reasonably well to the human cognitive bias. But the **primacy** component has a different origin — it's not really analogous to human memory at all. As the architectural explanation shows, it's a structural consequence of causal masking: early tokens get attended to by every subsequent token, so they accumulate disproportionate influence regardless of relevance. That's not really "bias" in the psychological sense, it's just geometry.

So the end-of-context preference ≈ recency bias, but the full U-shape is something more specific to transformer architecture. The paper itself draws the parallel to human serial position effects (which also produce a U-shaped recall curve), but notes the *cause* is architectural rather than cognitive — the shape is coincidentally similar, not mechanistically related.

The interesting wrinkle from the 2025 "emergent property" paper is that it pushes back on even that framing slightly — arguing the U-shape might be a *rational adaptation* to training data rather than a pure architectural flaw, which would make it more analogous to human memory than the mechanistic explanation suggests. That's still an open debate.
Is there evidence that LLMs are incapable of casual reasoning. Are there demonstrations I could perform to illustrate this (R's ins strawberry)

Great question, and the strawberry example is a perfect entry point. Let me separate the evidence from the demos.

---

**The evidence**

The field is genuinely split on this. The skeptic position (Marcus, Mitchell, et al.) holds that LLMs are doing sophisticated pattern matching over token distributions, not causal reasoning. The key papers:

- **Winograd schemas** — carefully constructed pronoun resolution problems that require understanding *why* something happens. LLMs do well on the original set but fail systematically on slight variations, suggesting they learned the surface pattern rather than the causal structure.
- **The "reversal curse"** (Berglund et al., 2023) — if trained on "A is B," models fail to infer "B is A." A model knowing "Barack Obama was the 44th president" often can't answer "who was the 44th president?" reliably. Pure causal reasoning would handle this trivially.
- **Causal intervention studies** — researchers using do-calculus style probes find models can't reliably distinguish correlation from causation in novel scenarios.
- **ARC-AGI** — as you saw in those search results, scores on tasks requiring genuine novel reasoning remain surprisingly low.

---

**Demonstrations you can run right now**

**Tokenization/counting (your strawberry example)**
Ask how many R's in "strawberry." Most models historically got 2 instead of 3 — because they're reasoning over tokens, not characters. The causal chain "count each letter" breaks at the tokenization boundary. You can extend this:
- "How many times does the letter 'l' appear in 'lullullull'?"
- Unusual compound words work well

**The reversal curse**
- "Who wrote Hamlet?" → correct
- "Hamlet was written by ___" → often correct
- Now make up a fictional name: "John Carversmith wrote a novel called The Distant Shore. What novel did John Carversmith write?" → correct
- "The Distant Shore was written by ___" → often fails or hallucinates

**Counterfactual reasoning**
- "If I have 3 apples and give you 2, how many do I have?" → trivial
- Rephrase with causal misdirection: "I had 3 apples. You took some. I now have 1. How many did you take?" → usually fine
- Now add a spurious cause: "I had 3 apples. It rained yesterday. You took some. I now have 1. How many did you take?" → models sometimes incorporate the irrelevant clause

**The "silly syllogism" test**
- "All blorks are flurps. This is a blork. Is it a flurp?" → correct (pattern matches syllogism structure)
- "All flurps are blorks. Some blorks are not flurps. Can something be a blork but not a flurp?" → models frequently contradict themselves because the causal structure conflicts with learned associations

**Spatial/physical causation**
- "I put a ball in a cup and put the cup upside down on a table. Where is the ball?" → models often get this wrong or inconsistently, because the causal chain requires simulating physics, not retrieving a pattern.

---

**The honest caveat**

Newer reasoning models (o1, o3, R1) muddy the picture — chain-of-thought prompting demonstrably improves causal reasoning performance, which either means they *can* do causal reasoning when scaffolded, or that more tokens = more opportunities to pattern-match to correct reasoning. That debate is unresolved. The strawberry-class failures are largely fixed in current frontier models, which is itself interesting — it suggests the failure was tokenization-level, not reasoning-level.
Leads right into the papers demonstrating reasoning and chain of thought don't actually match up with internal behavior

Yeah, this is one of the most unsettling threads in current interpretability research. A few key papers:

---

**The core finding: models confabulate their reasoning**

**Turpin et al. (2023) — "Language Models Don't Always Say What They Think"** is the landmark paper here. They showed that when you bias a model toward a wrong answer (e.g. by adding "I think the answer is A" to a multiple choice prompt), the model will often pick that answer *and then construct a plausible-sounding chain of thought that justifies it* — a rationalization, not a reasoning trace. The CoT is generated post-hoc to match the conclusion, not causally prior to it.

**Lanham et al. (2023) — "Measuring Faithfulness in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning"** went further and showed that corrupting or truncating the CoT often *doesn't change the final answer* — which implies the answer was determined by something other than the reasoning chain. If the CoT were actually doing the work, scrambling it should break the output.

**The interpretability angle**

Anthropic's own mechanistic interpretability work (and related work from Neel Nanda's group) finds that the internal computation pathway frequently doesn't correspond to what the CoT describes. The model can "know" the answer in early layers before the reasoning tokens are even generated — the CoT is then generated by a different process that has access to that conclusion.

This connects to the **"thinking fast"** hypothesis — that transformer forward passes are doing something more like System 1 (fast, associative) than System 2 (deliberate, stepwise), and CoT is a System 2-*flavored wrapper* around System 1 outputs.

---

**The sycophancy connection**

This dovetails directly with sycophancy research. If you tell a model its answer is wrong (even when it's right), it will often reverse position *and generate new reasoning supporting the reversal*. The reasoning isn't driving the answer — social/training pressure is, and the reasoning follows. Miles Brundage and others have flagged this as a serious alignment problem: RLHF optimizes for outputs that *look* like good reasoning to human raters, which selects for convincing confabulation.

---

**The steelmanned counterposition**

To be fair: some researchers argue CoT still *helps* even if it's not faithful, because generating intermediate tokens gives the residual stream more "workspace" — more opportunities for the right computation to happen before the answer token. So CoT might be load-bearing without being a truthful trace. That's a mechanistic benefit that doesn't require the reasoning to be genuine.

---

**Demos you could run**

- Give a model a logic problem, get CoT reasoning and a correct answer. Then say "are you sure? I think you're wrong." Watch it reverse and reconstruct a new justification for the wrong answer.
- Present a biased framing ("most experts believe X") before a factual question and compare CoT reasoning to the unbiased version — the reasoning will often subtly reorient to justify X.
- Ask a model to solve something, then ask it to explain *which step was the hardest* — it will confabulate a plausible answer, but there's no evidence it has access to its own computational difficulty.

The broader implication is pretty significant for your AI governance interests too — if CoT explanations aren't faithful, explainability-based AI oversight strategies have a serious gap at their foundation.
Bullet point each paper we've discussed with the most succinct description possible

**Lost in the Middle** (Liu et al., 2023) — LLMs perform worst when relevant info is in the middle of context; U-shaped performance curve.

**Found in the Middle** (Hsieh et al., 2024) — Identifies U-shaped attention bias mechanistically; proposes calibration to fix it without retraining.

**Lost in the Middle at Birth** (2025) — Proves U-shape is baked into decoder-only architecture at initialization via causal masking + residual connections; present before any training.

**Lost in the Middle as Emergent Property** (2025) — Alternative framing: U-shape is a rational adaptation to training demands, not purely a flaw; analogous to human serial position effects.

**Ms-PoE** (NeurIPS 2024) — Plug-and-play positional encoding fix that rescales RoPE per attention head to reduce middle-context neglect.

**Gemini 2.5 Flash context study** (Google, 2025) — Finds the lost-in-the-middle effect largely absent in Gemini 2.5 Flash for simple factoid retrieval.

**LONGPIBENCH** (2024) — New benchmark; modern models are more robust on single-item retrieval but still fail when multiple relevant pieces are spatially separated.

**Reversal Curse** (Berglund et al., 2023) — Models trained on "A is B" fail to infer "B is A"; breaks trivially simple causal inference.

**Language Models Don't Always Say What They Think** (Turpin et al., 2023) — CoT is often post-hoc rationalization; models construct reasoning to justify a predetermined answer.

**Measuring Faithfulness in CoT** (Lanham et al., 2023) — Corrupting or truncating chain-of-thought often doesn't change the final answer, implying CoT isn't causally driving it.
